ai-winters.A ban on linear algebra: Linear algebra is fundamental to AI and was never banned; funding cuts occurred due to unmet expectations.
ai-winters.The invention of GPUs: GPUs actually helped end AI winters by accelerating compute; they did not cause them.
ai-winters.Models becoming too accurate: AI winters occurred because models were too brittle and failed, not because they were too accurate.

imagenet-impact.It invented SQL: SQL was invented in the 1970s for relational databases; ImageNet is a computer vision dataset from 2009.
imagenet-impact.It ended the need for labeled data: ImageNet is a highly labeled dataset; it proved the power of supervised learning at scale.
imagenet-impact.It was only a chatbot dataset: ImageNet consists of millions of labeled images, not text for conversational chatbots.

alexnet-significance.Symbolic AI had solved natural language: AlexNet was a deep learning model for computer vision, unrelated to symbolic natural language processing.
alexnet-significance.Tiny rule systems beat all neural models: AlexNet proved that deep neural networks outclassed traditional rule-based and shallow computer vision systems.
alexnet-significance.Transformers had replaced CNNs: AlexNet was a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN); Transformers were not invented until 2017.

scaling-laws.A legal rule for AI copyright: Scaling laws are empirical scientific observations of performance trends, not legal regulations.
scaling-laws.A guarantee of perfect reasoning: Scaling laws predict lower loss/perplexity, but do not guarantee reasoning accuracy or eliminate hallucinations.
scaling-laws.A method for shrinking every model to ze: Scaling laws describe the effects of increasing parameters and data, not shrinking models to zero.

data-quality-pretraining.The model ignores all text quality: Pretrained models learn statistical patterns directly from data, meaning they absorb errors and noise.
data-quality-pretraining.Only the file extension matters: The semantic content and cleanliness of the data matter, not just file formats or extensions.
data-quality-pretraining.Quality matters only after deployment: Data quality issues during pretraining shape core behaviors that are hard to correct post-deployment.

tokenizer-role.It verifies facts automatically: Tokenizers only split text into numeric sequences; they do not perform fact-checking.
tokenizer-role.It stores user passwords: Tokenizers process prompt text in memory and do not serve as password storage databases.
tokenizer-role.It replaces the neural network: A tokenizer is a preprocessing tool that prepares input text for the neural network.

tokenization-surprise.Numbers are never tokenized: Numbers are tokenized, but often into erratic sub-word parts that make math harder for the model.
tokenization-surprise.Rare words are always deleted: Rare words are kept but split into smaller, sometimes confusing sub-word pieces.
tokenization-surprise.Tokenization runs only after the answer: Tokenization must run first to convert the input text into a format the model can read.

instruction-tuning.Training only on image pixels: Instruction tuning is applied to text models to improve natural language understanding and assistant capabilities.
instruction-tuning.Compressing a vector database: Instruction tuning alters model weights, whereas vector databases are external document stores.
instruction-tuning.Removing all safety data: Instruction tuning typically includes safety examples to align model behavior and enforce guardrails.

lora-purpose.Making prompts shorter by magic: LoRA modifies model parameter updates during training; it does not compress prompt text.
lora-purpose.Replacing the tokenizer with SQL: LoRA is a parameter-efficient tuning method for neural weights, not a database or tokenizer replacement.
lora-purpose.Guaranteeing no overfitting: While LoRA reduces parameters, overfitting can still occur if the dataset is too small or narrow.

overfitting-finetune.The model becomes unable to tokenize: Overfitting affects generalization and generation quality, not the tokenizer's capability.
overfitting-finetune.The GPU becomes a vector database: A GPU is hardware for compute; overfitting does not alter hardware roles.
overfitting-finetune.The model can no longer answer any old t: The model might lose some general capabilities (catastrophic forgetting), but not all capabilities by definition.

rlhf-purpose.Training the first tokenizer: Tokenizers are trained unsupervised before pretraining; RLHF is a post-pretraining alignment step.
rlhf-purpose.Increasing hard drive space: RLHF updates model parameters to change output behavior, not to increase storage space.
rlhf-purpose.Replacing all evaluation: RLHF is a training method, not an evaluation framework; rigorous evaluations are still required.

reward-model.It executes tools: A reward model evaluates text quality; it does not connect to or execute external tools.
reward-model.It stores embeddings: A reward model outputs scalar scores; it does not store semantic vectors like a database.
reward-model.It generates the training corpus from no: A reward model scores existing model completions; it does not create the initial pretraining text.

rlhf-limit.It prevents all jailbreaks: Jailbreaks can bypass RLHF safeguards using adversarial prompts or roleplay.
rlhf-limit.It removes the need for system prompts: System prompts are still essential to set context, instructions, and rules.
rlhf-limit.It makes models deterministic forever: RLHF models still sample tokens probabilistically unless temperature is set to zero.

dpo-vs-rlhf.It needs no preference data: DPO still requires preference data; it simply avoids training a separate reward model.
dpo-vs-rlhf.It is a vector database: DPO is a loss formulation for weight optimization, not an external vector search engine.
dpo-vs-rlhf.It only works for image models: DPO is heavily used and was designed for aligning language models.

rag-basic.Training a model from scratch on every p: RAG retrieves existing documents at query time; it does not train the model from scratch.
rag-basic.A method for deleting context: RAG adds relevant context to the prompt, rather than deleting it.
rag-basic.A benchmark for GPU fans: RAG is a software pattern for document retrieval, not a hardware stress test.

rag-not-training.They are exactly identical: RAG provides context in the prompt; fine-tuning updates the neural network's weights.
rag-not-training.RAG always requires model weight updates: RAG operates on frozen weights by feeding retrieved documents into the context window.
rag-not-training.Fine-tuning only stores PDFs: Fine-tuning updates parameters using text datasets; it does not store PDF documents.

rag-chunking.Chunks make embeddings unnecessary: Chunking is done prior to generating embeddings so that vectors represent coherent text blocks.
rag-chunking.Chunks encrypt the model weights: Document chunking is a preprocessing step for prompt injection, unrelated to model encryption.
rag-chunking.Chunk size only affects button colors: Chunk size determines the amount and relevance of context loaded into the prompt.

rag-citations.To make answers longer automatically: Citations are meant for verification and debugging, not simply to increase length.
rag-citations.To avoid storing documents: Documents must still be stored to show the source content when referenced.
rag-citations.To train GPUs: Citations are processed at inference time and are not used to train hardware accelerators.

rag-failure-mode.The database always answers perfectly: Retrieval databases can fail to find relevant records or return outdated/incorrect documents.
rag-failure-mode.The model stops using tokens: The model still processes text using tokens regardless of whether retrieval succeeds.
rag-failure-mode.Embeddings become human-readable prose: Embeddings are numeric vectors and remain abstract representations.

embedding-definition.A plain-text password: Embeddings are arrays of numbers representing meaning; they are not credentials.
embedding-definition.A Telegram command: Embeddings are numeric arrays used in vector space, not chatbot commands.
embedding-definition.A hand-written grammar rule: Embeddings are learned statistical vectors, not hand-coded rule templates.

embedding-similarity.How many files are in SQLite: Cosine similarity measures vector direction angles, not database file counts.
embedding-similarity.How expensive a GPU is: Cosine similarity is a geometric calculation, unrelated to hardware pricing.
embedding-similarity.Whether the answer is legally approved: Similarity checks find semantically related text; they do not perform legal compliance checks.

embedding-model-choice.All embedding models are identical: Models vary in vector dimensions, language coverage, and domain specialization.
embedding-model-choice.Only model file size matters: A small model trained on specialized domains can outperform a large general-purpose model.
embedding-model-choice.Embedding models cannot handle text: Embedding models specifically convert input text into numeric vectors.

embedding-drift.SQLite requires it by law: Re-indexing is a technical compatibility requirement, not a legal database regulation.
embedding-drift.The documents disappear: The text documents remain; only the numeric search vectors must be regenerated.
embedding-drift.It makes LLMs stop hallucinating: Re-embedding vectors improves search quality, but does not stop model hallucinations.

vector-db-purpose.Rendering Telegram buttons: Telegram UIs are rendered by application code, not vector database queries.
vector-db-purpose.Replacing all application code: Vector databases handle similarity searches; standard application code is still needed.
vector-db-purpose.Charging a model battery: LLMs do not have physical batteries; they run on standard servers.

ann-search.It guarantees every answer is factual: ANN search retrieves relevant documents; it does not check if the model's response is true.
ann-search.It removes indexing: ANN requires creating specialized indexes (like HNSW or IVF) to search quickly.
ann-search.It only works for tiny lists: ANN search is specifically designed for very large scales where exact search is too slow.

metadata-filtering.To make tokens free: Metadata filtering is a search constraint; it does not change API or token computation costs.
metadata-filtering.To convert vectors to images: Metadata filtering applies logical constraints (e.g. date > 2024), unrelated to image generation.
metadata-filtering.To prevent all prompt injection: Metadata filtering secures data boundaries but does not stop adversarial instructions in prompts.

agent-definition.It has a cartoon avatar: An agent is defined by its reasoning loops and tool use, not its visual presentation.
agent-definition.It only answers one fixed FAQ: Agents are dynamic and solve multi-step problems, unlike static FAQ reply scripts.
agent-definition.It must always run on a robot: Most agents run entirely as software on standard web servers.

agent-loop.Compile, delete, sleep, forget: These are random operations, not a structured reasoning-action loop.
agent-loop.Tokenize once and stop forever: Agents process text dynamically at each step, tokenizing multiple times as needed.
agent-loop.Train a foundation model from scratch ea: Agents call existing models; they do not retrain models from scratch at each step.

agent-risk.They never use context: Agents rely heavily on context to decide their next steps.
agent-risk.They cannot call tools: Calling external tools is a defining characteristic of AI agents.
agent-risk.They are always offline: Agents often require network access to execute tools and query APIs.

human-in-loop.Before reading its own prompt: Prompt reading is a safe, internal operation that does not require human approval.
human-in-loop.For every token it generates: Confirming every single token would make the agent unusable and incredibly slow.
human-in-loop.Only after deployment is over: Approval is needed during live operation to prevent real-world mistakes.

tool-use-basic.To make it conscious: Tools provide functional capabilities, not consciousness or self-awareness.
tool-use-basic.To avoid all prompts: Tools are triggered and guided by prompts; they do not eliminate them.
tool-use-basic.To remove the need for permissions: Tool use increases the need for strict execution permissions and boundaries.

tool-schema.They make hallucinations impossible: The model can still hallucinate arguments even if the schema is strictly defined.
tool-schema.They replace authentication: Schemas define data formats; they do not handle security credentials or authentication.
tool-schema.They are only decorative: Tool schemas are parsed by the model to generate correctly formatted arguments.

tool-result-grounding.Ignore it and invent an answer: Ignoring tool outputs defeats the purpose of giving the agent tools in the first place.
tool-result-grounding.Delete the user message: Deleting user inputs would break the conversation flow entirely.
tool-result-grounding.Retrain itself immediately: Agents process results in the context window; they do not retrain their neural weights.

tool-safety.Give every tool admin access: Admin access increases the severity of mistakes; least privilege is the safe pattern.
tool-safety.Hide all logs: Hiding logs prevents auditing and makes debugging agent behavior impossible.
tool-safety.Let prompts override permissions: Prompts are untrusted inputs and must not override security boundaries.

eval-heldout.To make the model larger: Evaluation data does not increase model parameters or size.
eval-heldout.To avoid measuring anything: Held-out data is used specifically to measure performance objectively.
eval-heldout.To teach the tokenizer: Tokenizers are trained on general corpora, not on validation/held-out sets.

eval-human.Automatic metrics are illegal: Automatic metrics are legal and widely used, but they lack nuance for subjective tasks.
eval-human.Humans can inspect model weights by sigh: Model weights are arrays of billions of numbers and cannot be read by sight.
eval-human.Benchmarks never change: Benchmarks change and evolve frequently as models become more capable.

eval-regression.Training only on regressions in statisti: Regression testing is a validation practice, not a model training methodology.
eval-regression.Deleting old prompts: Deleting test prompts prevents verifying that historical features still work.
eval-regression.Measuring GPU fan speed: Hardware performance is monitored, but regression testing evaluates software output quality.

eval-leakage.The benchmark server gets dusty: Benchmark contamination refers to data leakage, not physical server conditions.
eval-leakage.The model refuses every prompt: Contamination causes models to answer too easily by memorization, not to refuse prompts.
eval-leakage.The tokenizer uses Unicode: Unicode support is standard for tokenizers and unrelated to benchmark data leakage.

benchmark-purpose.To certify moral perfection: Benchmarks measure technical performance, not human moral standards.
benchmark-purpose.To replace product testing: Benchmarks show general capability but do not replace product-specific testing.
benchmark-purpose.To store user settings: User settings are saved in application databases, not capability benchmarks.

benchmark-limit.They are always fake: Benchmarks are real datasets, but they can be gamed and might not reflect your specific use case.
benchmark-limit.They measure only electricity: Benchmarks test language, coding, or logic capabilities, not power consumption.
benchmark-limit.They cannot be gamed: Benchmarks can be gamed through dataset contamination or specialized fine-tuning.

mmlu.Speech synthesis naturalness: MMLU is a multiple-choice text benchmark, not an audio synthesis test.
mmlu.Database write speed: Database performance is a software engineering metric, unrelated to MMLU.
mmlu.Telegram message delivery: MMLU tests model knowledge, not chatbot message routing.

coding-benchmark-limit.Benchmarks cannot contain code: Many benchmarks (like HumanEval) consist entirely of coding puzzles.
coding-benchmark-limit.Agents never use tools: Coding agents rely heavily on tools like terminals and file editors.
coding-benchmark-limit.All repositories are identical: Codebases vary widely in dependencies, styles, and architecture.

context-window.A browser tab title: Context window is the capacity of the model's active memory, not a browser feature.
context-window.A database backup: A database backup is a persistent data dump, unrelated to active inference memory.
context-window.A GPU brand: GPUs are hardware accelerators; context capacity is an architectural limit.

context-not-memory.Long context changes the model weights: Context window inputs are processed in-memory and do not update model weights.
context-not-memory.The model remembers every user forever a: Conversations are forgotten as soon as the context window is cleared.
context-not-memory.Memory means larger buttons: Memory relates to data persistence, not UI layouts or button designs.

lost-in-middle.The tokenizer crashes on commas: Tokenizers handle punctuation easily; 'lost in the middle' is an attention issue.
lost-in-middle.The model always stops halfway: The model still completes generation, but might ignore details in the middle of its prompt.
lost-in-middle.The database loses rows: Database integrity is managed by DBMS and is unaffected by context window dynamics.

context-cost.Models reject all long prompts: Models can accept long prompts, but they cost more and slow down inference.
context-cost.Tokens are unlimited: Token processing is constrained by compute limits and carries financial costs.
context-cost.It automatically improves every answer: Noisy or irrelevant documents in context can distract the model and worsen the answer.

hallucination-definition.A model seeing images in dreams: Models do not sleep or dream; hallucinations are simply ungrounded text generations.
hallucination-definition.A required part of tokenization: Tokenization splits text into segments, which is a deterministic process.
hallucination-definition.A database index: An index accelerates database lookups, unrelated to model generation errors.

hallucination-cause.They are connected to too many monitors: Display monitors do not affect the internal probabilistic calculations of LLMs.
hallucination-cause.They cannot generate grammar: LLMs are excellent at grammar; the issue is that they generate fluent but false claims.
hallucination-cause.SQLite forces them to: SQLite is a storage tool and does not dictate model output content.

reduce-hallucination.Ask for more confident wording: Demanding confidence often leads to more fluent and convincing hallucinations.
reduce-hallucination.Remove all citations: Removing citations makes verification impossible for the user.
reduce-hallucination.Use only random sampling: Random sampling increases output variety but does not guarantee factuality.

hallucination-eval.By counting adjectives: Adjective count does not correlate with semantic truth or accuracy.
hallucination-eval.By checking if the answer is long: Long answers can easily contain detailed, fluent lies.
hallucination-eval.By measuring keyboard speed: Keyboard speed is a user client metric, unrelated to output factuality.

safety-alignment.Making all models the same size: Alignment is about behavioral control, not model parameters or dimensions.
safety-alignment.Centering text on a page: Centering text is a CSS layout detail, not AI safety alignment.
safety-alignment.Encrypting embeddings only: Encryption secures data storage, while alignment guides model behavior.

prompt-injection.A way to add RAM: Prompt injection is a security vulnerability, not a hardware memory upgrade.
prompt-injection.A normal optimizer step: Optimizer steps update model weights during training; injection happens at query time.
prompt-injection.A vector compression format: Prompt injection is an adversarial attack pattern, not a data compression format.

jailbreak.A database migration: Jailbreaking is a safety bypass technique, unrelated to database schema updates.
jailbreak.A GPU cooling method: Jailbreaking is software steering, not hardware thermal management.
jailbreak.A tokenizer merge rule: Tokenizer merge rules define how subwords are grouped, unrelated to safety filters.

data-privacy.Models cannot process private text: Models process any text fed into them; the issue is keeping that text secure.
data-privacy.Privacy applies only to images: Text prompts often contain credentials, medical history, or proprietary IP.
data-privacy.Tokens erase secrets automatically: Tokens are numeric representations of text and preserve the underlying secrets.

open-source-models.They are always free to use commercially: Many open-weight models have commercial limits or usage restrictions in their licenses.
open-source-models.They always beat closed models: Top closed models often exceed open models in scale and broad reasoning capabilities.
open-source-models.They need no hardware: Open models must still be hosted and run on local or cloud accelerators.

model-license.Licenses change tokenizer output: Licenses are legal agreements and do not alter tokenizer logic.
model-license.Licenses are only for images: Model licenses apply to model weights, codebases, and generated outputs.
model-license.All open models share one license: Open weights use a variety of custom licenses (e.g. Llama 3, Apache 2.0, Gemma).

distillation.Compressing PDFs into vectors only: Distillation is a neural network training technique, not PDF text extraction.
distillation.Deleting training data: Distillation requires training data to match the teacher's outputs on target prompts.
distillation.Replacing prompts with SQL: Distillation changes model weights, not prompt formats or database queries.

quantization.Increasing all weights to infinite preci: Quantization decreases weight precision (e.g. 16-bit to 4-bit) to save memory.
quantization.Turning text into images: Quantization is a model compression method, not text-to-image synthesis.
quantization.Guaranteeing better reasoning: Quantization reduces memory requirements, but usually leads to small quality losses.

local-llm-benefit.To avoid needing any CPU: Local inference still requires a CPU (and ideally a GPU/NPU) to run.
local-llm-benefit.To make the model know today's news auto: Models only know what is in their training data unless connected to live search tools.
local-llm-benefit.To remove evaluation needs: Local models still need evaluation to check accuracy, latency, and task fit.

local-llm-limit.They cannot tokenize text: Local models run the same tokenizers as cloud models to process input text.
local-llm-limit.They require public internet for every t: A primary benefit of local models is that they can run completely offline.
local-llm-limit.They are always more capable than hosted: Local models are typically smaller due to consumer hardware limitations.

multimodal-definition.A model with multiple usernames: Multimodal models process different data types (vision/audio), not usernames.
multimodal-definition.A SQL database with two tables: Databases store structured records; they are not multimodal neural networks.
multimodal-definition.A model trained only on plain text: Unimodal text models process only text; multimodal models accept other types.

vision-language.Only sort database rows: Vision-language models handle image understanding, not database indexing.
vision-language.Only play audio: Vision-language models process visual inputs, not speaker outputs.
vision-language.Never use text: VLMs use text prompts to ask questions about images and output text answers.

ocr-vs-vlm.It cannot read text: VLMs are highly capable of reading text within images (visual document understanding).
ocr-vs-vlm.It only stores vectors: VLMs output natural language text, not just numeric vectors.
ocr-vs-vlm.It is always smaller than OCR: OCR engines are usually lightweight, while VLMs are large generative models.

coding-agent-definition.A model that only writes poems: Coding agents are designed for software engineering tasks, not literature writing.
coding-agent-definition.A compiler flag: A compiler compiles code; a coding agent is an autonomous assistant.
coding-agent-definition.A database migration: Database migrations change schemas; coding agents write and modify codebase files.

coding-agent-tests.Tests make code private: Tests verify code correctness; they do not encrypt or secure the repository.
coding-agent-tests.Tests replace reading code: Agents must still read and understand code to make edits that pass tests.
coding-agent-tests.Tests guarantee perfect UX: Tests check logical contracts, not user experience or design aesthetics.

coding-agent-diff.Diffs are only for images: Diffs are standard code comparisons showing line additions and deletions.
coding-agent-diff.Reviewing diffs deletes bugs automatical: Reviewing diffs helps human developers find mistakes, but does not fix them.
coding-agent-diff.It replaces all tests: Diff reviews and unit tests complement each other; one does not replace the other.

repo-context.All repos use identical architecture: Repos vary significantly in design patterns, style conventions, and packages.
repo-context.Context is only needed for comments: Context is crucial to write functional code that interfaces with existing systems.
repo-context.It makes syntax optional: Syntax rules are determined by the language compiler/interpreter and remain mandatory.

swe-bench.Whether a chatbot can sing: SWE-bench measures software engineering task success, not audio capabilities.
swe-bench.Whether embeddings are encrypted: SWE-bench evaluates issue resolution, not vector database security configurations.
swe-bench.Whether GPUs are quiet: Hardware fan noise is a hardware design detail, unrelated to software agent benchmarks.

temperature.The physical GPU temperature only: Temperature is a probabilistic token scaling parameter, not hardware heat.
temperature.The number of database rows: Temperature does not alter database contents or record counts.
temperature.The model's license: Temperature is a generation setting, unrelated to legal usage agreements.

top-p.Always picks the longest word: Top-p samples from a cumulative probability set; it does not check word length.
top-p.Deletes low-quality datasets: Top-p is an inference sampling setting, not a training data filter.
top-p.Trains preference models: Top-p is used at query time; it is not a training optimizer.

beam-search.It cannot generate text: Beam search is a generation algorithm; it is highly capable of creating text.
beam-search.It requires a vector database: Beam search works directly on token probabilities during model generation.
beam-search.It only works for images: Beam search is heavily used in machine translation and structured text generation.

system-prompt.Storing model weights: System prompts are text instructions fed into the context window, not parameter arrays.
system-prompt.Encrypting vectors: System prompts guide text generation; they do not encrypt database records.
system-prompt.Measuring benchmark leakage: Benchmark leakage must be checked using overlap analysis, not prompts.

structured-output.It improves every answer's truth automat: A model can output false or hallucinated data in JSON format just as easily as text.
structured-output.It removes the need for schemas: Structured output requires schemas to specify the desired JSON fields and types.
structured-output.It makes text unreadable to the model: Structured constraints are processed by the sampler and remain readable.

function-calling.Making all tools public: Function calling is a model output mode; it does not modify tool access configurations.
function-calling.Replacing the event loop: Function calling provides the arguments; the application event loop still runs them.
function-calling.Avoiding authentication: Authentication is managed by the application layer and is still required.

agent-memory.All secrets by default: Storing secrets in durable agent memory increases security exposure risks.
agent-memory.Only random thoughts: Memory should store actionable facts and choices to support long-term goals.
agent-memory.Nothing after user consent: Memory is preserved across runs to help the user; deleting it completely reduces usefulness.

planning-limit.It eliminates tool errors: Errors from tools can still happen and might compound if the agent's plan is brittle.
planning-limit.It makes permissions unnecessary: Long autonomous planning increases the necessity of strict permissions.
planning-limit.It prevents all hallucinations: The model can still hallucinate steps in the plan if it lacks grounding.

reranking.Training the base model from scratch: Reranking is a lightweight post-retrieval sorting step; it does not train models.
reranking.Changing Telegram settings: Reranking is a search relevance step, unrelated to chatbot configurations.
reranking.Compressing GPU memory: Rerankers process text inputs and do not optimize GPU memory layouts.

hybrid-search.Using two Telegram bots: Hybrid search relates to document index lookups, not chatbot instances.
hybrid-search.Mixing Python and SQL syntax randomly: Hybrid search combines keyword and vector algorithms, not programming languages.
hybrid-search.Deleting metadata filters: Metadata filters are still crucial to scope hybrid search results.

embedding-dimensions.Telegram command names: Vector dimensions are numeric widths, unrelated to chatbot commands.
embedding-dimensions.Whether a model can use tools: Tool capability is determined by prompts and code, not embedding sizes.
embedding-dimensions.The user's streak: User streaks are tracked in the database, independent of vector search spaces.

vector-index-rebuild.Every time a user clicks a button: Rebuilding indexes is expensive and should only be done when data or metrics change.
vector-index-rebuild.Only when the model says hello: Index maintenance is triggered by data updates, not model greeting tokens.
vector-index-rebuild.Never under any circumstances: Index rebuilds are required when changing embedding dimensions or distance metrics.

safety-evals.Only benchmark speed: Evaluations test behavioral safety and policy compliance, not hardware speeds.
safety-evals.Only GPU cost: Safety checks evaluate outputs, not operational server hosting expenses.
safety-evals.Only UI colors: Interface colors are design details, unrelated to model safety policies.

red-teaming.Painting the UI red: Red-teaming is adversarial safety testing, not interface styling.
red-teaming.Compressing model weights: Red-teaming tests model boundaries; it does not compress weights.
red-teaming.Sorting embeddings alphabetically: Embeddings are numeric arrays and cannot be sorted alphabetically.

calibration.Centering text on screen: Calibration refers to probability correctness, not page layouts.
calibration.Making all outputs short: Calibration measures confidence accuracy, independent of text length.
calibration.Encrypting benchmark data: Calibration is a statistical evaluation, not data security.

latency-throughput.Only accuracy matters: Slow responses can ruin user experience even if the outputs are accurate.
latency-throughput.Latency is never visible: Latency is directly experienced by users as the delay before generation starts.
latency-throughput.Throughput replaces safety: Operational speed does not make safety filters less important.

batching-inference.To make outputs private by default: Batching groups requests for efficiency; it does not encrypt or secure them.
batching-inference.To reduce all prompts to one token: Batching does not shorten individual prompts or change their token count.
batching-inference.To skip tokenization: All batched prompts must still be tokenized before model processing.

kv-cache.Saving user passwords: KV cache stores intermediate attention states in RAM, not user credentials.
kv-cache.Training embeddings: KV cache speeds up generation at query time; it is not used in training embeddings.
kv-cache.Replacing all context: KV cache retains prior context attention keys; it does not replace the context window.

attention-complexity.It uses no matrix operations: Attention relies heavily on large matrix multiplications.
attention-complexity.It stores only one token: Attention compares all tokens in the sequence pairwise, storing relations for all.
attention-complexity.It cannot run on GPUs: Attention is highly parallel and runs efficiently on GPUs, though memory demands are high.

encoder-decoder.Only storing embeddings: Encoder-decoder models generate output sequences; they are not static embedding stores.
encoder-decoder.Only running Telegram bots: Bots are software scripts; encoder-decoder is a model architecture.
encoder-decoder.Only replacing SQLite: Encoder-decoder is a neural network model, not a relational database.

decoder-only.They cannot generate text: Decoder-only models are the primary architecture used for text generation.
decoder-only.They require no pretraining: Decoder-only models still require scale pretraining to learn language and logic.
decoder-only.They only classify images: Decoder-only architectures are heavily optimized for natural language text.

moe-models.A group chat of human experts: MoE is an algorithmic neural architecture, not a human discussion group.
moe-models.A vector database table: MoE directs token paths inside model layers; it is not a database search index.
moe-models.A tokenizer with no vocabulary: MoE models use standard tokenizers to map text inputs.

catastrophic-forgetting.Forgetting the API token: Catastrophic forgetting affects model parameters, not application credentials.
catastrophic-forgetting.Deleting the SQLite file: SQLite deletions are database actions, unrelated to model parameter updates.
catastrophic-forgetting.A vector index crash: Vector index failures are database issues, not neural network alignment losses.

eval-golden-set.A model license type: Golden sets are curated test sets, not legal distribution agreements.
eval-golden-set.A GPU benchmark only: Golden sets evaluate model accuracy and behavior, not hardware compute speeds.
eval-golden-set.A prompt style with emojis: Golden sets represent test cases, not conversational styles.

leaderboard-overfit.Using too little RAM: Overfitting to leaderboards is a training evaluation bias, not a RAM limit.
leaderboard-overfit.Never submitting results: Overfitting occurs because teams optimize specifically to submit higher scores.
leaderboard-overfit.Running local models: Local model execution is an operational choice, unrelated to leaderboard bias.

multimodal-safety.Images cannot contain information: Images contain visual data that models interpret, including adversarial instructions.
multimodal-safety.Audio is always safe: Audio inputs can contain voice command injections or harmful sounds.
multimodal-safety.Multimodal models cannot use tools: Multimodal agents can call tools based on visual and textual instructions.

speech-models.Only database indexing: Speech models process audio signals, not database database rows.
speech-models.Only vector compression: Speech models transcribe or generate audio; they do not compress vectors.
speech-models.Only code formatting: Speech models deal with spoken sound waves, unrelated to code layout tools.

study-review-priority.Wrong answers should be deleted: Wrong answers must be tracked to schedule them for review.
study-review-priority.Only easy items should repeat: Easy items should repeat less often to save user time for harder topics.
study-review-priority.Review timing does not matter: Review timing is critical to reinforce memory before forgetting occurs.

weak-question-learned.Immediately after one wrong answer: One wrong answer identifies a weak question; it does not mark it learned.
weak-question-learned.Only after changing settings: Seeding settings does not verify user knowledge of specific questions.
weak-question-learned.Never: Questions are marked learned after repeated correct answers to keep the review pool focused.
